Option C cannot be derived from Rutherford's $\alpha$-particle scattering experiment.

Rutherford's experiment led to several conclusions about the structure of the atom:

Option A: Most of the space in the atom is empty. This conclusion was drawn because most of the $\alpha$-particles passed through the gold foil without any deflection, indicating that atoms are mostly empty space.

Option B: The radius of the atom is about $10^5$ times the radius of the nucleus. This was inferred from the fact that very few $\alpha$-particles were deflected back, suggesting a tiny, dense nucleus compared to the overall size of the atom.

Option D: Nearly all the mass of the atom resides in the nucleus. This conclusion was reached because the deflection of some $\alpha$-particles implied a very dense center, which contains most of the atomic mass.

Option C refers to the description of electron orbits, which was developed later by Niels Bohr. Bohr's model introduced the concept of electrons in specific orbits with fixed energies, an idea not covered or implied by Rutherford's scattering experiment.

title: NDA General Ability 18 April 2021
question: Twinkling of stars is due to
options: reflection of starlight from the oceanic surface
solution:
The phenomenon of twinkling of stars is due to the atmospheric refraction of starlight.

When starlight enters the Earth's atmosphere, it passes through layers of air at different temperatures and densities. As light travels through these varying layers, it is continuously refracted, or bent, in different directions. This refraction causes the light of the star to fluctuate in intensity and position as observed from Earth, leading to the perception of the star "twinkling." This effect is more pronounced for stars near the horizon compared to those directly overhead because the light path through the atmosphere is longer and passes through more layers of varying density.

title: NDA General Ability 18 April 2021
question: Which one of the following cannot be the unit of frequency of a sound wave?
solution:
Frequency is defined as the number of occurrences of a repeating event per unit of time. The standard unit of frequency is the hertz (Hz), which is equivalent to one cycle per second.

In terms of units that can express frequency:

s-1 (inverse seconds) is equivalent to Hz, as it indicates cycles per second.

Hz is the SI unit for frequency, representing cycles per second.

min-1 (inverse minutes) is a valid unit for frequency, representing cycles per minute.

dB (decibel) is a unit used to express the intensity or level of sound, not the frequency. Therefore, decibels cannot be used as a unit of frequency.

So, the unit that cannot be the unit of frequency of a sound wave is dB (decibel).

title: NDA General Ability 18 April 2021
question: 'Beats' is a phenomenon that occurs when frequencies of two harmonic waves are
solution:
The phenomenon of "beats" occurs when the frequencies of two harmonic waves are nearly the same.

When two waves of slightly different frequencies interfere with each other, they create a phenomenon known as beats. The resulting sound frequency that is heard is the average of the two frequencies, while the amplitude of the sound oscillates at the difference of the two frequencies. The beat frequency can be expressed mathematically as:

$$ f_{\text{beat}} = |f_1 - f_2| $$

where $ f_1 $ and $ f_2 $ are the frequencies of the two waves. This oscillation in amplitude leads to a pulsing effect, which is the characteristic sound associated with beats.

title: NDA General Ability 18 April 2021
question: Light waves are incident on an air-glass boundary. Some of the light waves are reflected and some are refracted in the glass.  Which one of the following properties is the same for the incident wave and the refracted wave?
solution:
The property that remains the same for the incident wave and the refracted wave is the frequency.

When light waves pass from one medium to another (e.g., from air to glass), the speed and direction of the wave can change due to the difference in optical density of the two media. However, the frequency of the light waves remains constant across the boundary.

In mathematical terms, if the incident wave has a frequency $$ f_i $$ and a wavelength $$ \lambda_i $$ in air, and the refracted wave has a frequency $$ f_r $$ and a wavelength $$ \lambda_r $$ in glass, then:

$$ f_i = f_r $$

This equality is a consequence of the principle that the oscillations per second (frequency) do not change as the wave enters a different medium, even though the wavelength and speed might change.

To explain further, the speed of a wave ($v$) is related to its frequency ($f$) and wavelength ($\lambda$) by the equation:

$$ v = f \lambda $$

When the wave enters a medium with a different refractive index, its speed changes according to:

$$ v_{\text{glass}} = \frac{c}{n} $$

where $ c $ is the speed of light in vacuum and $ n $ is the refractive index of the glass.

The change in speed leads to a change in wavelength while maintaining the same frequency:

$$ \lambda_{\text{glass}} = \frac{v_{\text{glass}}}{f} $$

Thus, the frequency is the invariant property across media transition.

title: NDA General Ability 18 April 2021
question: Which one of the following statements is true for a simple harmonic oscillator?
options: Force acting is directly proportional to the displacement from the mean position and is in same direction.
Force acting is directly proportional to the displacement from the mean position and is in opposite direction
Acceleration of the oscillator is constant
The velocity of the oscillator is not periodic
solution:
The force acting on a simple harmonic oscillator is described by Hooke's Law, which states that the force is proportional to the displacement from the equilibrium position and acts in the opposite direction. Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$ F = -kx $$

where:

$ F $ is the restoring force,

$ k $ is the spring constant,

$ x $ is the displacement from the mean position.

This negative sign indicates that the force acts in the opposite direction of the displacement. Therefore, the correct statement is:

Option B: Force acting is directly proportional to the displacement from the mean position and is in opposite direction.

Other reasons why Option B is correct:

Option A contradicts the nature of restoring force in simple harmonic motion because the direction of force should be opposite to the displacement.

Option C is incorrect as the acceleration is not constant; it is maximum at maximum displacement and zero at the mean position.

Option D is incorrect since the velocity in simple harmonic motion is indeed periodic, following a sine or cosine function based on time.

title: NDA General Ability 18 April 2021
question: Which one of the following colours may be obtained by combining green and red colours?
solution:
Combining green and red colors creates yellow in the RGB color model, which is used based on the additive color mixing process. In this model, the primary colors are red, green, and blue, and the secondary colors are formed by the combination of two primary colors. Here's a breakdown of how the colors combine:

Red + Green results in Yellow.

Green + Blue results in Cyan.

Blue + Red results in Magenta.

Thus, the correct option is:

Option D: Yellow

title: NDA General Ability 18 April 2021
question: Which of the following are the primary colours of light?
solution:
The primary colors of light are identified based on the additive color model, where different colors of light are mixed to create other colors. In this model, the primary colors are:

Option B: Blue, Red, and Green

These colors can be combined in various ways to produce a wide spectrum of colors, including white when all are combined at full intensity. This model is the same principle used in devices like computer screens, televisions, and cameras, where different intensities of red, green, and blue light are combined to produce the wide array of colors we perceive.

title: NDA General Ability 18 April 2021
question: According to the New Cartesian Sign Convention, which one of the following is correct is respect of the formula $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{v}+ \dfrac{1}{u}$, where symbols have their usual meanings?
options: It applies only to spherical mirrors
It applies only to spherical lenses
It applies to spherical mirrors as well as spherical lenses
solution:
In the New Cartesian Sign Convention, the formula for a spherical mirror is

$ \frac{1}{f} \;=\; \frac{1}{u} \;+\; \frac{1}{v}, $

where $f$ is the focal length of the mirror, $u$ is the object distance, and $v$ is the image distance.

However, for a thin spherical lens, the corresponding formula (under the same sign convention) is

$ \frac{1}{f} \;=\; \frac{1}{v} \;-\; \frac{1}{u}. $

Thus, the given formula

$ \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u} $

holds only for spherical mirrors, and not for lenses under the New Cartesian Sign Convention. Therefore, the correct choice is:

Option A: It applies only to spherical mirrors.

title: NDA General Ability 18 April 2021
question: Which one of the following statements is not correct for light rays?
options: Light travels at different speeds in different media
Light travels at almost 300 million metres per second in air
Light speeds down as it leaves a water surface and enters the air
Light speeds up as it leaves a glass surface and enters the air
solution:
The statement that is not correct is Option C: Light speeds down as it leaves a water surface and enters the air.

Here's why:

Light travels at different speeds in different media because the refractive index of each medium is different. The refractive index (n) is a measure of how much a medium slows down light compared to its speed in a vacuum ($c \approx 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$). The speed of light in a medium ($v$) is given by:

$$ v = \frac{c}{n} $$

When light travels from a denser medium (like water) to a rarer medium (like air), its speed increases. This is because the refractive index of water is greater than that of air. The phenomenon of light changing speed and bending at the boundary between two media is called refraction. Snell's Law describes the relationship between the angles of incidence and refraction and the refractive indices of the two media:

$$ n_1 \sin(\theta_1) = n_2 \sin(\theta_2) $$

where:

$n_1$ and $n_2$ are the refractive indices of the first and second media, respectively.

$\theta_1$ is the angle of incidence.

$\theta_2$ is the angle of refraction.

Therefore, as light leaves water and enters air, its speed increases (it speeds up), not decreases (slows down). Option D correctly reflects this behavior. Option A is true, as speed depends on the refractive index of the medium. Option B is approximately true; light's speed in air is very close to its speed in a vacuum.

title: NDA General Ability 18 April 2021
question: A glass prism splits white light into different colours. This phenomenon is called dispersion of light by prism. Which one of the following statements is correct?
options: Red light will deviate the most and it is because of the reflection of light
Violet light will deviate the most and it is because of the refraction of light
Red light will deviate the most and it is because of the refraction of light
Violet light will deviate the most and it is because of the reflection of light
solution:
Option B is correct: Violet light will deviate the most and it is because of the refraction of light.

When white light passes through a glass prism, it is dispersed into its constituent colors due to refraction. Each color of light bends at a different angle because each color has a different wavelength and refractive index. Violet light, having the shortest wavelength, is refracted the most, while red light, having the longest wavelength, is refracted the least. This differential bending of light waves causes the splitting of white light into its spectral colors, known as dispersion.

title: NDA General Ability 18 April 2021
question: A current of 1.0 A is drawn by a filament of an electric bulb for 10 minutes. The amount of electric charge that flows through the circuit is
solution:
The amount of electric charge ($Q$) that flows through a circuit can be calculated using the formula:

$$ Q = I \times t $$

where $I$ is the current and $t$ is the time in seconds.

Given:

Current ($I$) = 1.0 A

Time ($t$) = 10 minutes

First, convert the time from minutes to seconds:

$$ t = 10 \text{ minutes} \times 60 \text{ seconds/minute} = 600 \text{ seconds} $$

Now, substitute the values into the formula:

$$ Q = 1.0 \, \text{A} \times 600 \, \text{s} = 600 \, \text{C} $$

The amount of electric charge that flows through the circuit is:

Option C: 600 C

title: NDA General Ability 18 April 2021
question: Which one of the following formulas does not represent electrical power?
solution:
The formula that does not represent electrical power is Option B:

$$ I R^2 $$

Electrical power can be represented by three valid formulas:

$$ P = I^2 R $$, where $P$ is power, $I$ is current, and $R$ is resistance.

$$ P = V I $$, where $P$ is power, $V$ is voltage, and $I$ is current.

$$ P = \frac{V^2}{R} $$, where $P$ is power, $V$ is voltage, and $R$ is resistance.

Option B, $I R^2$, does not conform to any of these standard power formulas.

title: NDA General Ability 18 April 2021
question: The sound created in a big hall persists because of the repeated reflections. The phenomenon is called
solution:
The persistence of sound in a large hall due to repeated reflections is known as Reverberation.

When sound waves are produced in an enclosed space such as a big hall, they reflect off surfaces like walls, ceilings, and floors. These reflections cause the sound to persist even after the original sound source has stopped producing sound. This prolongation of sound is termed reverberation. It is a common effect in auditoriums and can be both beneficial and detrimental depending on the acoustic design of the space. Excessive reverberation can make speech unintelligible, while controlled reverberations can enrich the sound of music.

Reverberation time (RT) is a key parameter used to measure this effect. It is defined as the time it takes for the sound to decay by 60 decibels after the sound source has stopped.

Mathematically, the reverberation time can be approximated by the Sabine formula:

$$ RT = \frac{{0.161 \times V}}{{A}} $$

where:

$ V $ is the volume of the room in cubic meters.

$ A $ is the total absorption in the room in square meters sabine.

Effective design of acoustic spaces often seeks to optimize reverberation to suit the primary purpose of the environment, such as speech, music, or general sound clarity.

title: NDA General Ability 18 April 2021
question: When light scattered by a molecule and the frequency of the scattered light is changed, this phenomenon is called
solution:
The phenomenon where light is scattered by a molecule and the frequency of the scattered light is changed is known as the Raman effect.

During Raman scattering, when a photon interacts with a molecule, it can either give energy to the molecule, raising its vibrational state (Stokes scattering), or take energy away from it, lowering its vibrational state (anti-Stokes scattering). This results in a frequency shift of the scattered light relative to the incident light.

The Raman effect is a form of inelastic scattering, named after Indian physicist C.V. Raman, who discovered this effect in 1928. It plays a crucial role in chemical analysis and molecular spectroscopy.

Option B: Raman effect.

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: Two resistances of 5.0 Ω and 7.0 Ω are connected in series and the combination is connected in parallel with a resistance of 36.0 Ω. The equivalent resistance of the combination of three resistors is
solution:
Step 1: Write down what is given.

  • Two resistances $R_1 = 5.0 \, \Omega$ and $R_2 = 7.0 \, \Omega$ are connected in series.

  • Their series combination is connected in parallel with $R_3 = 36.0 \, \Omega$.

Step 2: Find the series resistance of $R_1$ and $R_2$.

$ R_{\text{series}} = R_1 + R_2 = 5.0 + 7.0 = 12.0 \, \Omega $

Step 3: Now, $R_{\text{series}} = 12.0 \, \Omega$ is in parallel with $R_3 = 36.0 \, \Omega$.

For two resistors in parallel:

$ \frac{1}{R_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{R_{\text{series}}} + \frac{1}{R_3} $

$ \frac{1}{R_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{12} + \frac{1}{36} $

$ \frac{1}{R_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{3 + 1}{36} = \frac{4}{36} = \frac{1}{9} $

Step 4: Take reciprocal to get the equivalent resistance.

$ R_{\text{eq}} = 9.0 \, \Omega $

Final Answer: Option 3 → 9.0 Ω

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: Shown in the figure are two hollow cubes C1 and C2 of negligible mass partially filled (depicted by darkened area) with liquids of densities ρ1 and ρ2, respectively, floating in water (density ρw). The relationship between ρ1, ρ2 and ρw is
options: ρ2 < ρ1 < ρw
ρ1 < ρw < ρ2
solution:
Key Idea:

When something floats, it means it is lighter (has a lower average density) than the liquid it is in.

For floating, the weight of water pushed aside by the object is equal to the weight of the object.

If an object sinks deeper (is more submerged), it means its average density is higher.

For hollow cubes with very small weight, the heaviness (density) of the cube comes only from the liquid that is inside them.

What the Figure Shows:

The figure has two cubes, C1 and C2, each filled with a different liquid. The densities of these liquids are ρ1 and ρ2.

C2 is deeper in the water than C1. This means C2 is heavier (has a greater density) than C1.

Because the cubes themselves do not weigh much, the only thing that affects how deep they float is the density of the liquid inside.

So, the density ρ1 (inside C1) is greater than ρ2 (inside C2).

Both cubes float. This tells us that the density of the liquids inside must be less than the density of water (ρw), or otherwise they would sink.

Putting all this together:

ρ2 < ρ1 < ρw

Answer: ρ2 < ρ1 < ρw

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: <!-- x-tinymce/html -->An astronaut whose weight on the Earth is 600 N experiences weightless- ness on International Space Station orbiting around the Earth. It means that
options: normal reaction of the space- station floor on the astronaut is zero
gravitational pull of earth on the astronaut is zero
space station applies a centrifugal force on the astronaut
solution:
1. On Earth, the weight of the astronaut is

    $$W = mg = 600\text{ N}\quad\bigl(m=\tfrac{W}{g}\bigr).$$

  2. In the International Space Station (ISS) both the station and the astronaut are in continuous free fall around the Earth. They have the same centripetal acceleration towards Earth.

  3. “Weightlessness” means the astronaut feels no contact force from the floor. In free fall:

    – Gravitational force $mg$ acts on the astronaut.

    – There is no normal reaction $N$ from the floor (because both fall together).

  4. Therefore:

    Option 2 is correct – the normal reaction of the space-station floor on the astronaut is zero.

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question:
Which of the following statements give characteristics of contact forces?

1. It appears between an object when it is in contact with some other object

2. It satisfies the third law of motion

3. It may appear between a pair of solid and fluid

Select the answer using the code given below:
solution:
1. Definition of contact force

    – A force that arises only when two bodies are in physical contact.

  2. Check statement (1): “It appears between an object when it is in contact with some other object”

    – This is exactly the definition of a contact force.

    ⇒ Statement 1 is correct.

  3. Check statement (2): “It satisfies the third law of motion”

    – Newton’s third law, “To every action there is an equal and opposite reaction,” holds for all forces, including contact forces.

    ⇒ Statement 2 is correct.

  4. Check statement (3): “It may appear between a pair of solid and fluid”

    – Examples:

    • Fluid pressure on a submerged plate (normal contact force)

    • Viscous drag between a fluid and a moving solid

    ⇒ Statement 3 is correct.

All three statements are true.

Answer: Option 4 (1, 2 and 3)

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: Which one among the. following figures correctly represents the ray diagram ? (Consider the lens to be thin)
solution:
CONCEPT:

Ray Diagrams for Lenses

A ray diagram is a drawing that shows how light rays move through a lens.

There are three main rays we usually draw for a thin lens:

First Ray: If a ray comes in parallel to the main (principal) axis, it will bend and pass through the lens's focus on the other side.

Second Ray: If a ray goes through the center of the lens, it travels straight and does not bend.

Third Ray: If a ray goes toward the focus before reaching the lens, it will bend after passing through the lens and move out parallel to the main axis.

EXPLANATION:

For a convex lens (one that bulges out):

- A ray that comes in parallel to the main axis will bend and go through the focus on the other side.

- A ray that travels through the middle of the lens goes straight and does not change direction.

- A ray that heads toward the focus before entering the lens will come out of the lens going parallel to the main axis.

So, the right figure for the ray diagram is Option 3.

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: Lightning is due to
options: The flow of charges between different parts of the cloud
The short-circuiting of charges between the upper and lower surfaces of the cloud
The collection of positively charged particles on the base and collection of negatively charged particles at the top of the cloud
The induction of positive charge on the ground below the negative charge at the base of the cloud
solution:
Option 1

  1. Kondensation and collision of water‐droplets and ice particles in a cumulonimbus cloud → frictional charging.

  2. Lighter ice crystals (positive) carried to the top, heavier water/ice (negative) settle at the base → large separation of charge.

  3. Potential difference between different regions of the cloud (or between cloud and ground) increases.

  4. When electric field exceeds air’s dielectric strength, a sudden discharge (spark) occurs – this is lightning.

  5. Thus lightning is essentially the rapid flow of charges between oppositely charged parts of the cloud.

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: In which one among the following situations, the bulb ⊗ would glow the most ? (Consider all batteries are the same)
solution:
CONCEPT:

Power in a Bulb and Types of Circuits

Power, which makes a bulb glow, is found with the formula:

P = V2 / R

Here, P is power, V is voltage, and R is resistance.

In a series circuit, the battery's voltage is split between all the parts. In a parallel circuit, each part gets the full battery voltage.

EXPLANATION:

The bulb shines the brightest when it gets as much voltage as possible because more voltage means more power (according to the formula above).

If the bulb is in a parallel circuit, it will get the full battery voltage. This makes it glow more.

If the bulb is in a series circuit with other things, the voltage is shared, so the bulb gets less voltage and glows less.

So, the bulb will glow the most if it is connected in parallel.

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: Which one among the following diagrams may correctly represent the motion of a skydiver during a jump ?
solution:
CONCEPT:

How a Skydiver Falls

Step 1: Skydiver Jumps

When a skydiver jumps out, gravity pulls them down. Their speed goes up fast at first because they are falling freely through the air.

Step 2: Reaching Terminal Velocity

As the skydiver falls faster, air pushes back harder. Eventually, the push from the air (air resistance) matches the pull of gravity, so the skydiver's speed stops increasing. This steady speed is called terminal velocity.

Step 3: Opening the Parachute

When the parachute opens, there is a sudden big increase in air resistance. This makes the skydiver slow down very quickly.

Speed-Time Graph:

The speed-time graph starts by going up quickly (as the skydiver speeds up). Then, it flattens out (terminal velocity). After the parachute opens, the line drops sharply as the skydiver slows down fast.

EXPLANATION:

Option 1:

This graph is correct. It shows speed going up fast, staying the same for a while (terminal velocity), and then dropping quickly after the parachute opens.

Option 2:

This option is incorrect because it makes the speed go to zero too fast, which doesn't happen in real skydiving.

Option 3:

This option is wrong because it shows speed going up and down in a straight line, as if the skydiver stops suddenly without a parachute. This is unrealistic.

Option 4:

This option almost matches what really happens, but it does not clearly show a flat part for terminal velocity and the slowing down isn't sudden enough when the parachute opens.

So, Option 1 is correct.

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: Which one of the following sketches correctly describes a lever of second class?
solution:
CONCEPT:

Lever of Second Class

A lever of the second class has three main parts:

  • The fulcrum is at one end. The fulcrum is the point where the lever pivots or turns.
  • The load (the thing you want to move or lift) is in the middle.
  • The effort (where you push or pull) is at the other end, opposite the fulcrum.

In a second-class lever:

  • The load is always between the fulcrum and where you apply effort.
  • The arm where you apply effort (effort arm) is always longer than the part holding the load (load arm), so you get a mechanical advantage greater than 1. This means it's easier to lift something.
  • Examples of second-class levers are wheelbarrows, nutcrackers, and bottle openers.

EXPLANATION:

When you look at sketches of different levers:

  • A second-class lever should always have:
    • Fulcrum at one end,
    • Load in the middle,
    • And effort at the other end.
  • The correct sketch must show the load between the fulcrum and the effort.

So, the sketch that correctly shows a lever of the second class is Option 2.

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: For an electric circuit given below, the correct combination of voltage (V) and current (I) is
solution:
CONCEPT:

Parallel Circuits and Ohm’s Law

When resistors are in parallel, the voltage is the same across all resistors.

To find the total current, first combine all resistors into a single "equivalent resistance." Then use Ohm’s Law: V = IR or I = V / R.

EXPLANATION:

Step 1: Find Equivalent Resistance

The resistors, 100 Ω, 200 Ω, and 300 Ω, are all connected in parallel.

The formula for parallel resistors: 1/Req = 1/100 + 1/200 + 1/300

Let’s add:
1/100 = 6/600
1/200 = 3/600
1/300 = 2/600

Add together: (6 + 3 + 2) / 600 = 11 / 600

So, Req = 600 / 11 ≈ 54.55 Ω

Step 2: Find the Voltage

Ohm’s Law says V = IR.

From the circuit, the current through the 100 Ω resistor is given as 3 A.

So, V = 100 × 3 = 300 V

Step 3: Find the Total Current

Now, use the voltage (300 V) to find the current through each resistor:

  • For 100 Ω: I = 300 / 100 = 3 A
  • For 200 Ω: I = 300 / 200 = 1.5 A
  • For 300 Ω: I = 300 / 300 = 1 A

Total current = 3 + 1.5 + 1 = 5.5 A

So, the correct answer is: Option 2) V = 300 V ; I = 5.5 A

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: A point object is placed at the centre of curvature of a spherical concave mirror. Which one among the fol- lowing would be the correct location of image formed?
options: Between the focal point and the centre of curvature
solution:
1. For a concave mirror of radius of curvature $R$, the focal length is

    $$f=\frac{R}{2}$$

  2. Mirror formula (Cartesian sign convention):

    $$\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}$$

    where

    • $u$ = object distance from pole (real object ⇒ $u>0$)

    • $v$ = image distance from pole (real image ⇒ $v>0$)

  3. Here the object is at the centre of curvature, so

    $$u=R$$

  4. Substitute into the mirror formula:

    $$\frac{1}{R/2}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{R}$$

    $$\frac{2}{R}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{R}$$

    $$\frac{1}{v}=\frac{2}{R}-\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{R}$$

    $$\therefore v=R$$

  5. Hence the image is formed at a distance $R$ from the pole, i.e. at the centre of curvature.

Answer: Option 2 – At the centre of curvature.

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: Which one among the following is correct for a person suffering from myopia?
options: The person can see near objects clearly
The person can see distant objects clearly
The person can neither see near objects nor distant objects clearly
solution:
1. Myopia (short-sightedness) means the eye’s optical power is too strong or the eyeball is too long.

  2. Parallel rays from a distant object focus in front of the retina, so distant objects appear blurred.

  3. Rays from a nearby object diverge more and are brought to focus on the retina, so near objects appear clear.

Correct option: 1. The person can see near objects clearly.

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: A vehicle starts moving along a straight line path from rest. In first t seconds it moves with an acceleration of 2 m/s<sup>2</sup> and then in next 10 seconds it moves with an acceleration of 5 m/s<sup>2</sup>. The total distance travelled by the vehicle is 550 m. The value of time t is
solution:
In the first $t$ seconds (acceleration $2\ \text{m/s}^2$):

• Distance $s_1 = \tfrac12\cdot2\cdot t^2 = t^2$

• Final speed $v_1 = 2t$

In the next 10 s (acceleration $5\ \text{m/s}^2$, initial speed $v_1$):

$ s_2 = v_1\cdot10 + \tfrac12\cdot5\cdot10^2 = 2t\cdot10 + \tfrac12\cdot5\cdot100 = 20t + 250 $

Total distance is 550 m, so

$ s_1 + s_2 = t^2 + (20t + 250) = 550 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t^2 + 20t - 300 = 0 $

Solve the quadratic:

$ t = \frac{-20 \pm \sqrt{20^2 - 4\cdot1\cdot(-300)}}{2} = \frac{-20 \pm \sqrt{1600}}{2} = \frac{-20 \pm 40}{2} $

The positive root is $t = 10$ s.

Answer: 10 s.

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: A pumpkin weighs 7.5 N. On sub- merging it completely in water, $\frac{3}{4}$L of water gets displaced. The acceleration due to gravity at the place where the pumpkin was weighed is 10 m/s2. Which one of the following is the correct value of the density of the pumpkin?
options: 100 kg/m3
1000 kg/m3
10000 kg/m3
solution:
Mass of the pumpkin

$$m=\frac{W}{g}=\frac{7.5\rm\;N}{10\rm\;m/s^2}=0.75\;kg$$

Volume of the pumpkin (equal to displaced water)

$$V=\tfrac{3}{4}\,L=0.75\times10^{-3}\;m^3=7.5\times10^{-4}\;m^3$$

Density

$$\rho=\frac{m}{V}=\frac{0.75\;kg}{7.5\times10^{-4}\;m^3}=1000\;kg/m^3$$

Answer: 1000 kg/m³ (Option 3).

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: <!-- x-tinymce/html -->An incandescent electric bulb converts 20% of its power consumption into light, and the remaining power is dissipated as heat. The bulb's filament has a resistance of 200 Ω and 2 A current flows through it. If the bulb remains ON for 10 h and the rate of electricity charge is Rs. 5/unit, then which among the following is the correct amount for the money spent on producing light?
solution:
Power input of the bulb

$$P = I^2R = (2\text{ A})^2 \times 200\ \Omega = 4\times200 = 800\text{ W} = 0.8\text{ kW}$$

Total energy consumed in 10 h

$$E_{\rm tot} = P\times t = 0.8\ \text{kW}\times10\ \text{h} = 8\ \text{kWh}$$

Total cost of electricity at Rs. 5 per unit (1 unit=1 kWh)

$$\text{Cost}_{\rm tot} = 8\ \text{kWh}\times5\ \text{Rs./kWh} = 40\ \text{Rs.}$$

Only 20% of that goes into light

$$\text{Cost}_{\rm light} = 0.20\times40\ \text{Rs.} = 8\ \text{Rs.}$$

Answer: Rs. 8 (Option 4).

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: Which one of the following statements best defines the concept of heat?
options: The transformation of energy from one form to another
The conversion of energy into mass and vice-versa due to temperature difference
The transfer of energy due to temperature difference
The change in volume of a substance with temperature
solution:
Option 1: The transformation of energy from one form to another.

This describes how energy can change its form, like chemical energy turning into electrical energy in a battery, or potential energy turning into kinetic energy as something falls. While heat can be involved in these transformations, this statement describes energy conversion in general, not specifically what heat is.

Option 2: The conversion of energy into mass and vice-versa due to temperature difference.

This touches on the concept of mass-energy equivalence, often represented by Einstein's famous equation $$E=mc^2$$. However, this isn't how we define heat in everyday thermodynamics. Heat isn't about energy converting into mass.

Option 3: The transfer of energy due to temperature difference.

Bingo! This is the most accurate definition. Heat is energy that flows from a warmer object or region to a cooler one because there's a difference in their temperatures. Think about holding an ice cube – heat flows from your warmer hand to the colder ice cube, making your hand feel cold as it loses energy.

Option 4: The change in volume of a substance with temperature.

This phenomenon is called thermal expansion (or contraction), and it's a result of temperature changes. When a substance heats up, its particles move more vigorously and usually spread out, causing an increase in volume. While related to temperature, it's not the definition of heat itself.

So, the statement that best defines the concept of heat is Option 3: The transfer of energy due to temperature difference.

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: The masses of two planets are in the ratio of 1:7. The ratio between their diameters is 2: 1. The ratio of forces which they exert on each other is
solution:
The gravitational force between two bodies is mutual: by Newton’s third law, the force that planet 1 exerts on planet 2 equals the force that planet 2 exerts on planet 1. No matter what their masses or sizes,

$$F_{12} \;=\; F_{21}\,. $$

So the ratio of the two forces is

1 : 1

(Option 3).

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: <!-- x-tinymce/html -->A car weighs 1000 kg. It is moving with a uniform velocity of 72 km/h towards a straight road. The driver suddenly presses the brakes. The car stops in 0.2 s. The retarding force applied on the car to stop it is
solution:
Convert the speed to m/s:

$$v = 72\ \mathrm{km/h} = 72\cdot\frac{1000}{3600}\ \mathrm{m/s} = 20\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

Compute the deceleration (since it goes from 20 m/s to 0 in 0.2 s):

$$a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{0 - 20}{0.2} = -100\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$$

Find the retarding force using Newton’s second law:

$$F = m\,a = 1000\ \mathrm{kg}\times(-100\ \mathrm{m/s^2}) = -100\,000\ \mathrm N$$

The magnitude is $100{,}000\ \mathrm N$, i.e. 100 kN.

Answer: Option 4, 100 kN.

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: There is a ball of mass 320 g. It has 625 J potential energy when released freely from a height. The speed with which it will hit the ground is
solution:
First convert the mass to kilograms:

m = 320 g = 0.32 kg

All the potential energy turns into kinetic energy at the bottom:

$$\tfrac12\,m\,v^2 = \text{PE} = 625\text{ J}$$

Solving for v:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2\cdot625}{0.32}} = \sqrt{3906.25} = 62.5\text{ m/s}$$

So the ball hits the ground at 62.5 m/s (Option 1).

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: Starting from rest a vehicle accelerates at the rate of 2 m/s<sup>2</sup> towards east for 10 s. It then stops suddenly. It then accelerates again at a rate of 4√2 m/s<sup>2</sup> for next 10 s towards south and then again comes to rest. The net displacement of the vehicle from the starting point is
solution:
Let’s break it into two legs:

Eastward leg

– Acceleration $a_1=2\rm\,m/s^2$, time $t_1=10\rm\,s$, from rest.

– Distance

$$ s_1=\tfrac12\,a_1\,t_1^2 =\tfrac12\cdot2\cdot10^2 =100\rm\,m. $$

Southward leg

– Starts from rest again, $a_2=4\sqrt2\rm\,m/s^2$, $t_2=10\rm\,s$.

– Distance

$$ s_2=\tfrac12\,a_2\,t_2^2 =\tfrac12\cdot4\sqrt2\cdot10^2 =200\sqrt2\rm\,m. $$

Treating east as $x$–axis and south as $y$–axis, the net displacement magnitude is

$$ R=\sqrt{s_1^2 + s_2^2} =\sqrt{100^2 + (200\sqrt2)^2} =\sqrt{10000 + 80000} =\sqrt{90000} =300\rm\,m. $$

Answer: 300 m (Option 3).

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: Which one among the following is the correct focal length of a combination of lenses of power 2.5 D and -2.0 D?
solution:
To find the focal length of two thin lenses in contact:

Add their powers:

$$P_{\rm total}=P_1+P_2=2.5\,\mathrm D+(-2.0\,\mathrm D)=0.5\,\mathrm D$$

Take the reciprocal to get the focal length:

$$f_{\rm total}=\frac{1}{P_{\rm total}}=\frac{1}{0.5\,\mathrm D}=2.0\ \text{m}$$

So the correct answer is +2.0 m (Option 3).

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: The AC mains domestic supply current in India changes direction in every
solution:
The domestic AC in India has a frequency of 50 Hz.

One full cycle period:

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50}\text{ s} = 0.02\text{ s}$$

It reverses direction every half-cycle:

$$\frac{T}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\times\frac{1}{50}\text{ s} = \frac{1}{100}\text{ s} = 0.01\text{ s}$$

Answer: Option 4, $\tfrac{1}{100}$ s.

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question: A block of mass 20 kg slides on a rough horizontal plane surface. Let the speed of the block at a particular instant is 10 m/s. It comes to rest after travelling a distance of 20 m. Which one of the following could be the magnitude of the frictional force?
solution:
Let’s use the work–energy principle. The loss in kinetic energy equals the work done by friction:

Initial kinetic energy

$$K_i = \tfrac12 m v^2 = \tfrac12 \times 20\,\text{kg} \times (10\,\text{m/s})^2 = 1000\,\text{J}.$$

Work done by friction

$$W_{\rm fric} = f \times s,$$

where $f$ is the frictional force and $s=20$ m is the stopping distance.

Equate work to energy change

$$f \,s = K_i \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f = \frac{1000\,\text{J}}{20\,\text{m}} = 50\,\text{N}.$$

Answer: 50 N (Option 4).

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question:
Given below are the four cases in which certain heat transfer is taking place:

1. Ice is melting in a glass full of water

2. Water is boiling in an open container

3. A metal rod is heated in a furnace

4. A cup of coffee is allowed to cool on a table

In which of the above cases, the Newton's Law of Cooling is applicable ?
solution:
Newton’s law of cooling applies when a body (with uniform temperature) exchanges heat by convection (or convection + radiation) with its surroundings, without any phase change. In formula form:

$$\frac{dT}{dt}=-k\bigl(T(t)-T_\infty\bigr)$$

where

• $T(t)$ is the object’s temperature at time $t$,

• $T_\infty$ is the ambient temperature,

• $k$ is a positive constant (proportional to the heat‐transfer coefficient).

Checking each case:

Ice melting in water

– Phase change at constant $0^\circ$C → temperature doesn’t change, so the simple $dT/dt$ law doesn’t apply.

Water boiling in an open container

– Phase change at $100^\circ$C → again constant temperature during boiling.

Metal rod heated in a furnace

– Often involves conduction along the rod and radiation at high temperatures; temperature is not uniform and there’s more than simple convection.

Cup of coffee cooling on a table

– No phase change, coffee cools by convection/radiation, roughly uniform temperature → fits Newton’s law.

Answer: Only case 4.

title: NDA General Ability 1st September 2024
question:
The figure given below shows the direction of the two forces P and Q acting on a skydiver :

Which one among the following statements is correct about the two forces?
options: Force P is caused by the gravity and force Q is caused by the friction
<!-- x-tinymce/html -->When the force P is bigger than the force Q, the speed of the skydiver remains the same
After the parachute opens, force P remains the same while force Q increases
After the parachute opens, force P decreases while force Q increases
solution:
Forces Acting on a Skydiver

When a skydiver jumps out of an airplane, two primary forces act on them: the force of gravity (P) and the force of air resistance or friction (Q).

The force of gravity (P) pulls the skydiver downward towards the Earth.

The force of air resistance (Q) acts upward in the opposite direction and increases with the skydiver's speed.

Explanation:

When the skydiver's parachute is opened, the following occurs:

Force P (gravity) remains unchanged because it depends on the mass of the skydiver and Earth's gravitational pull, which do not change.

Force Q (air resistance) increases significantly as the parachute expands, increasing the surface area and thereby increasing air resistance.

Thus, after the parachute opens, force P remains the same while force Q increases.

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: An infinite combination of resistors, each having resistance $R = 4 \Omega$, is given below. What is the net resistance between the points A and B? (Each resistance is of equal value, $R = 4 \Omega$)
solution:
Equivalent Resistance,

$$ \begin{array}{rlrl} y =\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{R}|| y \\\\ \Rightarrow y =\mathrm{R}+\frac{\mathrm{R} \times y}{\mathrm{R}+y} \\\\ \Rightarrow y =\frac{\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{R} y+\mathrm{R} y}{\mathrm{R}+y} \\\\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{R} y+y^2 =\mathrm{R}^2+2 \mathrm{R} y \\\\ \Rightarrow y^2-4 y-16 =0 \\\\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}} \text { or } y =2+2 \sqrt{5} \text { ohm } \end{array} $$

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question:
An electric circuit is given below. $V_1 = 1 V$ and Resistance $R = 1000 \Omega$.

The current through the resistance $R$ is very close to $1$ mA and the voltage across point A and B, $V_{AB} = 1 V$. Now the circuit is changed to :

where value of $V_2 = 5 V$. The internal resistances of both the batteries are $0.1 \Omega$. The current through the resistance $R$ is about :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} \text { Equivalent voltage } & =\frac{\varepsilon_1 r_1+\varepsilon_2 1_2}{r_1+r_2} \\\\ & =\frac{1 \times 0.1+5 \times 0.1}{0.1+0.1} \\\\ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{eq}} & =\frac{0.6}{0.2}=3 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

From first circuit, we have

$$ i=\frac{\varepsilon}{R+r} $$

Value of $r$ is very less can be neglected as compared to $\mathrm{R}$

Now,

$$ \begin{aligned} i & =\frac{\varepsilon}{R} \\\\ 10^{-3} & =\frac{1}{R} \\\\ \mathrm{R} & =10^3=1000 \mathrm{ohm} \end{aligned} $$

Now, from second circuit

$$ \begin{aligned} i_1 & =\frac{\mathrm{E}_{e q}}{\mathrm{R}+r_{e q}}=\frac{3}{1000} \\\\ i_1 & =3 \times 10^{-3}=3 \mathrm{~mA} \end{aligned} $$

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: If the current through an electrical machine running on direct current is $15 A$ and the machine runs for $10$ minutes, the charge that passes through the machine during this time is:
solution:
To determine the charge that passes through the machine, we use the relationship between current, charge, and time. The formula that relates these quantities is given by :

$$ Q = I \times t $$

where :

$$ Q $$ is the charge (in coulombs, C)

$$ I $$ is the current (in amperes, A)

$$ t $$ is the time (in seconds, s)

We are given :

Current, $$ I = 15 \, \text{A} $$

Time, $$ t = 10 \, \text{minutes} $$

First, we need to convert the time from minutes to seconds :

$$ 10 \, \text{minutes} \times 60 \, \text{seconds per minute} = 600 \, \text{seconds} $$

Now, substituting the given values into the formula, we get :

$$ Q = 15 \, \text{A} \times 600 \, \text{s} $$

$$ Q = 9000 \, \text{C} $$

Therefore, the charge that passes through the machine during this time is :

Option D : $$ 9000 \, \text{C} $$

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: Which one of the following is the best shape of a solid metal rod to form the top end of a lightning conductor?
solution: Lightning rods are always pointed to attract charges towards it.

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question:
Which of the following forces is/are fundamental in nature?

1. Gravitational force

2. Electromagnetic forces

3. Strong and weak nuclear forces

Select the correct answer using the code given below :
solution:
In the realm of physics, fundamental forces, also known as fundamental interactions, are the basic forces that govern the interactions between particles and objects. There are four recognized fundamental forces in nature :

1. Gravitational Force: This is the force of attraction that acts between all masses. Despite being the weakest of the four forces, it has an infinite range and is always attractive. It's described by Newton's law of gravitation and Einstein's general theory of relativity.

2. Electromagnetic Forces: These forces act between charged particles and are described by Coulomb's law and Maxwell's equations. They have an infinite range and include electric forces (between stationary charges) and magnetic forces (between moving charges).

3. Strong Nuclear Force: This is the force that holds the protons and neutrons together in an atom's nucleus. Despite its extremely short range (approximately the size of an atomic nucleus, $$10^{-15}$$ meters), it is the strongest of the four fundamental forces.

4. Weak Nuclear Force: This force is responsible for certain types of particle decay processes, such as beta decay in radioactive atoms. Its range is even smaller than that of the strong nuclear force, approximately $$10^{-18}$$ meters, and it is weaker than both the strong nuclear and the electromagnetic forces.

Given this information, the correct answer is :

Option D : 1, 2 and 3

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: A spherical shell of outer radius R and inner radius R/2 contains a solid sphere of radius R/2 (see figure). The density of the material of the solid sphere is ρ and that of the shell is ρ/2. What is the average mass density of the larger sphere thus formed?
solution:
To determine the average mass density of the larger sphere formed by a spherical shell with an outer radius $ R $ and an inner radius $ R/2 $, containing a solid sphere of radius $ R/2 $, follow these steps:

The density of the solid sphere is $ \rho $, while the density of the shell is $ \rho/2 $. The average density can be computed using the formula:

$ \text{Average density} = \frac{\text{Total mass}}{\text{Total volume}} $

Breaking this down:

  1. Volume of the solid sphere (inner part):

$ V_1 = \frac{4}{3} \pi \left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^3 $

  1. Volume of the outer shell:

$ V_{\text{shell}} = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 - V_1 $

  1. Volume of the entire larger sphere (outer radius $ R $):

$ V = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 $

  1. Mass of the solid sphere:

$ m_1 = \rho \times V_1 $

  1. Mass of the shell:

$ m_{\text{shell}} = \frac{\rho}{2} \times V_{\text{shell}} $

Now, substitute these into the average density formula:

$ \text{Average density} = \frac{m_1 + m_{\text{shell}}}{V} $

Calculating step-by-step:

  1. Calculate $ V_1 $:

$ V_1 = \frac{4}{3} \pi \left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi \frac{R^3}{8} $

  1. Calculate $ V_{\text{shell}} $:

$ V_{\text{shell}} = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 - \frac{4}{3} \pi \frac{R^3}{8} = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \left(1 - \frac{1}{8}\right) = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \frac{7}{8} $

  1. Calculate $ m_1 $:

$ m_1 = \rho \times \frac{4}{3} \pi \frac{R^3}{8} $

  1. Calculate $ m_{\text{shell}} $:

$ m_{\text{shell}} = \frac{\rho}{2} \times \left( \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \frac{7}{8} \right) $

Combining these:

$ \text{Average density} = \frac{\rho \times \frac{4}{3} \pi \frac{R^3}{8} + \frac{\rho}{2} \times \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \frac{7}{8}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3} $

Simplify the equation:

$ = \frac{\rho \frac{R^3}{8} + \frac{\rho}{2} \times \frac{7 R^3}{8}}{R^3} $

$ = \frac{\rho}{8} + \frac{\rho}{2} \times \frac{7}{8} = \frac{\rho}{8} + \frac{7 \rho}{16} $

Combine the terms:

$ = \frac{2 \rho + 7 \rho}{16} = \frac{9 \rho}{16} $

Thus, the average mass density of the larger sphere formed is $\frac{9\rho}{16}$.

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: The human eye is like a camera that has a lens with :
options: fixed focal length and fixed aperture size.
variable focal length and fixed aperture size.
fixed focal length and variable aperture size.
variable focal length and variable aperture size.
solution:
The human eye functions similarly to a camera in many ways. When considering the characteristics of the eye's lens, we need to factor in its focal length and aperture size.

The focal length of the lens in the human eye is not fixed. The eye can change its focal length to focus on objects at different distances. This process is called accommodation, where the ciliary muscles adjust the shape of the lens to change its focal length.

Similarly, the aperture size of the human eye, which is the pupil, is variable. The size of the pupil changes in response to light intensity. In dim light, the pupil dilates to allow more light into the eye, and in bright light, it constricts to reduce the light entering the eye. This process is known as the pupillary light reflex.

Therefore, the correct answer is :

Option D : variable focal length and variable aperture size.

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: A uniform meter scale of mass 0.24 kg is made of steel. It is kept on two wedges, W₁ and W₂, in a horizontal position. W₁ is at a distance of 0.2 m from one of its ends, while W₂ is at a distance of 0.4 m from the other end. If the force on the scale is N₁ due to W₁ and N₂ due to W₂, then : (take g = 10.0 m s⁻²)
solution:
To solve this problem, we need to consider both the conditions of equilibrium: the translational equilibrium (sum of vertical forces must be zero) and rotational equilibrium (sum of torques about any point must be zero).

Let's denote:

  • N₁ as the force exerted by wedge W₁
  • N₂ as the force exerted by wedge W₂
  • mg as the weight of the meter scale

Given:

  • Mass of meter scale, m = 0.24 kg
  • Acceleration due to gravity, g = 10.0 m/s²
  • W₁ is at a distance of 0.2 m from one end
  • W₂ is at a distance of 0.4 m from the other end

First, let’s find the weight of the meter scale:

The weight, $$mg = 0.24 \, \text{kg} \times 10.0 \, \text{m/s}^2 = 2.4 \, \text{N}$$

Next, for translational equilibrium, the sum of vertical forces must be zero:

$$N₁ + N₂ = mg$$

So,

$$N₁ + N₂ = 2.4 \, \text{N} \quad \text{(Equation 1)}$$

For rotational equilibrium, we need to consider the torques about any point. We choose the point where one of the forces is applied to simplify the calculations. Let’s take torques about the point where W₁ is placed.

Distance of center of mass of the meter scale from W₁:

The center of mass for a uniform meter scale is at its midpoint, i.e., 0.5 m from either end. Therefore, the distance of the center of mass from W₁ (at 0.2 m from the end) is:

$$0.5 \, \text{m} - 0.2 \, \text{m} = 0.3 \, \text{m}$$

Torque due to the weight of the meter scale about W₁:

$$\tau_w = mg \times 0.3 \, \text{m} = 2.4 \, \text{N} \times 0.3 \, \text{m} = 0.72 \, \text{Nm}$$

Distance of W₂ from W₁:

The total distance W₂ is from W₁:

$$1 \, \text{m} - 0.2 \, \text{m} - 0.4 \, \text{m} = 0.4 \, \text{m}$$

Torque due to the force N₂ about W₁:

$$\tau_{N₂} = N₂ \times 0.4 \, \text{m}$$

Applying rotational equilibrium (sum of torques about W₁ equals zero):

$$0.72 \, \text{Nm} = N₂ \times 0.4 \, \text{m}$$

Therefore,

$$N₂ = \frac{0.72 \, \text{Nm}}{0.4 \, \text{m}} = 1.8 \, \text{N}$$

Substitute the value of N₂ back into Equation 1 to find N₁:

$$N₁ + 1.8 \, \text{N} = 2.4 \, \text{N}$$

Therefore,

$$N₁ = 2.4 \, \text{N} - 1.8 \, \text{N} = 0.6 \, \text{N}$$

So, the forces are:

  • $$N₁ = 0.6 \, \text{N}$$
  • $$N₂ = 1.8 \, \text{N}$$

The correct answer is:

Option C: N₁ = 0.6 N and N₂ = 1.8 N

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: Escape speed from the Earth is close to 11.2 km s⁻¹. On another planet whose radius is half of the Earth's radius and whose mass density is four times that of the Earth, the escape speed in km s⁻¹ will be close to :
solution:
To find the escape speed from another planet given its radius and mass density in relation to Earth's, we need to use the concept of escape velocity. The escape velocity $ v_e $ from a spherical body is given by:

$$ v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}} $$

where:

  • $ G $ is the gravitational constant
  • $ M $ is the mass of the planet
  • $ R $ is the radius of the planet

We know the escape speed from Earth is 11.2 km/s. Therefore:

$$ v_{e, \text{Earth}} = 11.2 \text{ km/s} $$

We denote the escape velocity of the new planet as $ v_{e, \text{planet}} $. Let's express the mass of the new planet in terms of Earth's mass.

The mass $ M $ of a planet can be written as the product of its volume and density:

$$ M = \rho V $$

For a spherical body:

$$ V = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 $$

Therefore:

$$ M \propto \rho R^3 $$

Given the new planet's radius $ R_p $ is half of Earth's radius $ R_e $:

$$ R_p = \frac{R_e}{2} $$

Also, its density $ \rho_p $ is four times the density of Earth $ \rho_e $:

$$ \rho_p = 4 \rho_e $$

The new planet's mass $ M_p $ compared to Earth's mass $ M_e $ can be written as:

$$ M_p \propto \rho_p R_p^3 = 4 \rho_e \left(\frac{R_e}{2}\right)^3 = 4 \rho_e \left(\frac{R_e^3}{8}\right) = \frac{\rho_e R_e^3}{2} = \frac{M_e}{2} $$

Thus:

$$ M_p = \frac{M_e}{2} $$

Now we substitute $ M_p $ and $ R_p $ into the escape velocity formula:

$$ v_{e, \text{planet}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 G M_p}{R_p}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 G \frac{M_e}{2}}{\frac{R_e}{2}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 G M_e}{R_e}} = \sqrt{\frac{G M_e}{R_e}} $$

This is because the factor of 2 in the denominator of the mass term and the factor of 2 in the radius term cancels out. So:

$$ v_{e, \text{planet}} = \sqrt{2} v_{e, \text{Earth}} $$

$$ v_{e, \text{planet}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot 11.2 \text{ km/s} $$

Calculating this value:

$$ v_{e, \text{planet}} = 11.2 \sqrt{2} \approx 11.2 \cdot 1.414 = 15.8 \text{ km/s} $$

Thus, the correct option is:\p>

Option B

15.8

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: Which one of the following does not apply to sound waves in fluids?
options: They travel faster in liquids than in gases
solution:
The option that does not apply to sound waves in fluids is:

Option C

They are transverse

Explanation:

Sound waves in fluids (liquids and gases) are longitudinal waves, not transverse waves. In longitudinal waves, the oscillations of the particles of the medium are parallel to the direction of wave propagation. This means that particles in the medium move back and forth in the same direction that the wave is traveling.

Let's break down the other options to see why they do apply to sound waves in fluids:

Option A: They transport energy

Sound waves indeed transport energy. As a sound wave propagates through a medium, it transfers energy from one particle to another through the medium.

Option B: They need a medium to travel

Sound waves require a medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to travel. They cannot propagate in a vacuum because there are no particles to transmit the vibrations.

Option D: They travel faster in liquids than in gases

Sound waves generally travel faster in liquids than in gases. This is because particles in liquids are more closely packed than in gases, allowing the sound waves to transmit vibrations more efficiently.

Therefore, Option C is the correct answer since sound waves in fluids are not transverse waves.

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: A ball of 0.1 kg mass is dropped on a hard floor from a height of 0.45 m and rises to a height of 0.2 m. If it was in touch with the floor for 0.1 s, the net force it applied on the floor while bouncing is : (take the gravitational acceleration g = 10 m s⁻²)
solution:
To determine the net force the ball applies on the floor while bouncing, we need to follow these steps:

Step 1: Calculate the velocity of the ball just before it hits the floor

Using the equation of motion:

$$ v^{2} = u^{2} + 2gh $$

where,

u = initial velocity (0 m/s, since the ball is dropped), g = acceleration due to gravity (10 m/s2), h = height from which the ball is dropped (0.45 m).

Therefore,

$$ v^{2} = 0 + 2 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.45 $$

$$ v^{2} = 9 $$

$$ v = \sqrt{9} $$

$$ v = 3 \, \text{m/s} $$

Step 2: Calculate the velocity of the ball just after it bounces off the floor

Using the same equation of motion, but this time with the final height the ball reaches:

$$ v^{2} = u^{2} + 2gh $$

where,

h = height ball rises to (0.2 m).

$$ v^{2} = 0 + 2 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.2 $$

$$ v^{2} = 4 $$

$$ v = \sqrt{4} $$

$$ v = 2 \, \text{m/s} $$

However, this velocity is upward (after the bounce). Thus, we should take it as negative for our momentum calculation to show the direction change.

Step 3: Calculate the change in momentum

Momentum before the bounce:

$$ p_{1} = mv = 0.1 \cdot 3 = 0.3 \, \text{kg m/s} $$

Momentum after the bounce:

$$ p_{2} = m(-v) = 0.1 \cdot (-2) = -0.2 \, \text{kg m/s} $$

Change in momentum:

$$ \Delta p = p_{2} - p_{1} = -0.2 - 0.3 = -0.5 \, \text{kg m/s} $$

Step 4: Calculate the net force

Using Newton's second law:

$$ F = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t} $$

where, Δt = time of contact (0.1 s).

Thus,

$$ F = \frac{-0.5}{0.1} = -5 \, \text{N} $$

The magnitude of the force is 5 N. However, we are interested in the net force applied on the floor, which is the positive value of this calculation.

Thus, the net force applied on the floor while bouncing is:

5.0 N

So, the correct answer is Option D: 5.0 N

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question:
Which one of the following statements regarding simple pendulum is correct?

Simple pendulum has a time period independent of amplitude:
options: only for small amplitudes because then the net force on its bob is independent of its displacement.
for any amplitude because the net force on the bob is always proportional to its displacement.
for any amplitude because the net force on the bob is independent of its displacement.
only for small amplitudes because then the net force on its bob is proportional to its displacement.
solution:
In order to accurately describe the behavior of a simple pendulum, we need to analyze its physics in detail. A simple pendulum consists of a mass (known as the bob) attached to a string of length $ L $ and fixed at a pivot point. When displaced from its equilibrium position and released, it oscillates back and forth under the influence of gravity.

The restoring force acting on the bob is provided by the component of the gravitational force that acts along the direction of the arc. This force can be expressed as:

$$ F = -mg \sin(\theta) $$

where:

  • F is the restoring force,
  • m is the mass of the bob,
  • g is the acceleration due to gravity,
  • θ is the angular displacement from the vertical.

For small angular displacements (small amplitudes), $ \sin(\theta) $ can be approximated by $ \theta $ (when $ \theta $ is measured in radians). Thus, the restoring force becomes:

$$ F \approx -mg\theta $$

Since $ \theta = \frac{x}{L} $ where $ x $ is the linear displacement and $ L $ is the length of the pendulum, the force can be rewritten as:

$$ F \approx -\frac{mg}{L} x $$

This shows that the restoring force is directly proportional to the displacement, characteristic of simple harmonic motion. The time period $ T $ of a simple pendulum is then given by:

$$ T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} $$

Note that this time period is independent of amplitude, but the approximation $ \sin(\theta) \approx \theta $ only holds for small amplitudes. Therefore, the correct statement regarding the simple pendulum is:

Option D: only for small amplitudes because then the net force on its bob is proportional to its displacement.

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: Which one of the following about different frictional forces is correct?
solution:
Let's examine and compare the different types of friction: static friction, kinetic friction, and rolling friction.

Static Friction : This is the friction that exists between a stationary object and the surface it's on. It needs to be overcome to start moving the object. It is generally the highest among the three because it involves overcoming the intermolecular forces between the surfaces.

Kinetic Friction : This is the friction that acts between moving surfaces. It is typically lower than static friction because once an object is in motion, less force is required to keep it moving compared to starting the motion.

Rolling Friction : This is the friction that occurs when an object rolls over a surface. Rolling friction is generally much smaller than both static and kinetic friction because the area of contact between the two surfaces is much smaller.

Therefore, the correct order from highest to lowest is generally :

$$\text{Static friction} > \text{Kinetic friction} > \text{Rolling friction}$$

Based on this understanding, the correct choice is :

Option C : Static friction > Kinetic friction > Rolling friction

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question:
A metallic bob X of mass m is released from position A. It collides elastically with another identical bob Y placed at rest at position B on a horizontal frictionless table. The angle AOB is 30°.

How high does the bob X rise immediately after the collision?
options: To the same height as that of position A on the other side in the same trajectory
To half the height as that of position A on the other side along the same trajectory
The same height at position A
solution:
To solve this problem, we need to consider the principles of conservation of momentum and conservation of energy. The collision between the two bobs is elastic, meaning both kinetic energy and momentum are conserved.

First, let's denote the initial height from which the bob X is released as $$h$$. When bob X is released from position A, it converts its potential energy into kinetic energy just before the collision :

$$mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Solving for the velocity $$v$$ just before collision :

$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

Since the bobs are identical (same mass $$m$$) and the collision is elastic, the velocities of the bobs after the collision can be derived using the following properties of an elastic collision: the moving object will come to rest, and the object at rest will move with the velocity of the moving object. Therefore, after the collision, the bob Y will move with the initial velocity of bob X, and bob X will come to a stop.

Because bob X comes to a stop immediately after the collision, it does not rise to any height after the collision. Hence, the correct answer is :

Option D : It stops at position B

question: Of the following, which does not belong to a nuclear reactor?
solution:
In a nuclear reactor, various components serve specific roles to ensure the reactor functions correctly. Let's analyze each option:

Option A: A turbine

A turbine is an essential part of the secondary circuit in a nuclear power plant. The primary circuit, where nuclear reactions occur, transfers its heat to a secondary circuit, which drives the turbine to generate electricity.

Option B: A heat exchanger

A heat exchanger is crucial in a nuclear reactor. It transfers the heat produced in the reactor core to the secondary circuit that drives the turbine. This component ensures the effective transfer of heat while maintaining the integrity of the radioactive material in the reactor core.

Option C: A mechanism to reduce CO2 emission

Nuclear reactors inherently do not produce CO2 emissions during their operation, as they rely on nuclear fission rather than fossil fuels. Therefore, a specific mechanism to reduce CO2 emissions is not a standard part of a nuclear reactor.

Option D: A reaction chamber

The reaction chamber, also known as the reactor core or vessel, is where the nuclear reactions occur. It contains the nuclear fuel and the control rods that regulate the fission process. This component is central to the operation of a nuclear reactor.

Given the above explanations, the component that does not belong to a nuclear reactor is:

Option C: A mechanism to reduce CO2 emission

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: A microscope may be a combination of :
options: a convex lens and a convex mirror.
solution:
A microscope is typically a combination of two convex lenses. The basic design of a simple microscope, also known as a magnifying glass, uses a single convex lens. However, compound microscopes, which are more common in scientific applications, use two convex lenses arranged in series to achieve higher magnifying power.

Each lens in a compound microscope serves a specific purpose:

Objective Lens: This is the lens closest to the object being viewed. It gathers light from the specimen and creates a enlarged real image.

Eyepiece Lens (Ocular Lens): This lens is closest to the observer's eye. It magnifies the real image created by the objective lens to create a larger virtual image that the observer can see.

The arrangement of these two lenses allows for greater magnification and resolution compared to a single lens. Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A: two convex lenses.

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question:
Which of the following statements with regard to the phenomenon of the primary rainbow formation by water droplets is/are correct?

1. It involves refraction and one internal reflection of sunlight.

2. It involves refraction of sunlight only.

3. It is formed as the inner bow.

4. It may involve more than one internal reflection as well as refraction of sunlight.

Select the answer using the code given below :
solution:
In order to understand the correct statements regarding the phenomenon of primary rainbow formation by water droplets, we need to delve into the physics of light behavior. A primary rainbow is a result of the interplay between refraction, reflection, and dispersion of sunlight within water droplets. Let's analyze the statements:

1. It involves refraction and one internal reflection of sunlight.

This statement is correct. When sunlight enters a water droplet, it bends (refracts). Inside the droplet, the light reflects off the inner surface and then refracts again as it exits the droplet. This process creates the spectrum of colors seen in a primary rainbow.

2. It involves refraction of sunlight only.

This statement is incorrect. As explained above, the formation of a primary rainbow involves both refraction and internal reflection, not just refraction alone.

3. It is formed as the inner bow.

This statement is correct. The primary rainbow is the inner bow, and its colors are visible in the sequence of red on the outer edge to violet on the inner edge.

4. It may involve more than one internal reflection as well as refraction of sunlight.

This statement is incorrect with regard to the primary rainbow but applies to secondary rainbows. A secondary rainbow is formed by two internal reflections within the water droplet, leading to a relatively fainter bow outside the primary rainbow with reverse color sequence.

Given this analysis, the correct statements are 1 and 3. Therefore, the answer is:

Option B:

1 and 3

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: Which one of the following scientists mentioned that an electron in an atom could revolve in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy?
solution:
The correct answer is Option C: Niels Bohr.

Niels Bohr was a pioneering physicist who made fundamental contributions to our understanding of atomic structure and quantum mechanics. In 1913, Bohr introduced his model of the atom, which incorporated some aspects of classical physics and early quantum theories. One of the key postulates of the Bohr model is that electrons can revolve around the nucleus of an atom in specific, quantized orbits without the emission of radiant energy. These stable orbits correspond to certain fixed energy levels.

Bohr proposed that while the electron remains in one of these discrete energy levels, no energy is radiated. Radiant energy is emitted or absorbed only when an electron transitions from one energy level to another. This idea was revolutionary because, according to classical electromagnetic theory, an accelerating charge such as an electron in orbit should continuously emit radiation and lose energy, leading to a collapse of the orbit. Bohr's model provided a way to circumvent this problem by introducing quantum concepts.

Bohr's theory can be summarized by the equation for the quantized angular momentum of an electron:

$$L = n \hbar$$

where:

  • $$L$$ is the angular momentum
  • $$n$$ is a positive integer (quantum number)
  • $$\hbar$$ is the reduced Planck's constant ( $$\hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi}$$ )

This discovery was critical in the development of quantum mechanics and helped to explain phenomena that could not be accounted for by classical physics alone. It laid the groundwork for future atomic models and a more comprehensive understanding of atomic and subatomic processes.

In conclusion, Niels Bohr's concept of electrons revolving in quantized stable orbits without radiating energy was fundamental in explaining atomic stability and the discrete nature of atomic spectra.

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: LIGO experiment confirmed one of the predictions of :
solution:
The LIGO (Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory) experiment confirmed one of the predictions of :

Option A : String theory

Option B : Special theory of relativity

Option C : Quantum mechanics

Option D : General theory of relativity

The correct answer is :

Option D : General theory of relativity

Explanation :

The LIGO experiment successfully detected gravitational waves, which are ripples in the fabric of spacetime caused by some of the most violent and energetic processes in the universe. This detection was a groundbreaking confirmation of Albert Einstein's General Theory of Relativity, which he published in 1915. Einstein's theory predicted the existence of gravitational waves, but they had never been directly observed until the LIGO experiment.

Gravitational waves are produced by accelerating masses, such as binary black holes or neutron star mergers. When these massive objects orbit each other and eventually collide, they generate waves that propagate through spacetime. The LIGO observatories were designed to detect these tiny distortions in spacetime as gravitational waves passed through the Earth.

The successful detection of gravitational waves by LIGO not only confirmed a major prediction of the General Theory of Relativity but also opened a new window for observing the universe, allowing scientists to study cosmic events that are otherwise invisible through conventional electromagnetic observation methods.

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: A block of mass 2 kg, moving with the initial speed of 3 m/s comes to rest on a rough horizontal surface after travelling a distance of 3 m. The magnitude of the frictional force is :
solution:
To find the frictional force acting on the block, we can use the work-energy principle. The principle states that the work done by all forces acting on an object is equal to the change in the object's kinetic energy.

First, let's denote the mass of the block as $ m $, the initial speed as $ v_i $, the final speed $ v_f $, the distance traveled $ d $, and the frictional force $ f_{fric} $.

Given :

  • Mass, $ m = 2 \, \text{kg} $
  • Initial speed, $ v_i = 3 \, \text{m/s} $
  • Final speed, $ v_f = 0 \, \text{m/s} $ (since the block comes to rest)
  • Distance traveled, $ d = 3 \, \text{m} $

The initial kinetic energy of the block is given by :

$$ KE_i = \frac{1}{2} m v_i^2 $$

Substituting the values :

$$ KE_i = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \, \text{kg} \times (3 \, \text{m/s})^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 9 = 9 \, \text{J} $$

The block comes to rest, so its final kinetic energy is zero :

$$ KE_f = 0 $$

The work done by the frictional force is equal to the change in kinetic energy of the block :

$$ W = KE_f - KE_i = 0 - 9 \, \text{J} = -9 \, \text{J} $$

The work done by the frictional force can also be calculated using :

$$ W = f_{fric} \times d $$

We know the distance $ d $ is 3 meters, so :

$$ -9 \, \text{J} = f_{fric} \times 3 \, \text{m} $$

Solving for the frictional force :

$$ f_{fric} = \frac{-9 \, \text{J}}{3 \, \text{m}} = -3 \, \text{N} $$

The magnitude of the frictional force is 3 N (the negative sign indicates the direction opposite to the motion). Therefore, the correct answer is :

Option B : 3 N

title: NDA General Ability 21 April 2024
question: Which one of the following is primarily responsible for conduction of current in a metal?
solution:
The correct answer is:

Option B

Free electrons

To understand this, let's delve into the nature of electrical conduction in metals. Metals are characterized by their unique atomic structure, which includes a lattice of positively charged ions surrounded by a 'sea' of free electrons. These free electrons are not bound to any particular atom and are free to move throughout the metal. This freedom of movement is a critical property that allows metals to conduct electric current efficiently.

When an electric field is applied to a metal, these free electrons are influenced by the field and start drifting in the direction opposite to the field. This movement of free electrons constitutes the electric current. The bound electrons, on the other hand, are tightly held by their respective atoms and cannot move freely to conduct current. Ions in a metal lattice are also stationary and do not contribute to the conduction of current. Therefore, it is primarily the free electrons that are responsible for the conduction of current in a metal.

For a quantitative understanding, we can refer to Ohm's Law, which states:

$$V = IR$$

Where:

$$V$$ is the voltage across the conductor,

$$I$$ is the current through the conductor,

$$R$$ is the resistance of the conductor.

In the context of metals, the low resistance is largely due to the high density of free electrons that can move with minimal opposition, thus allowing a significant current to flow with a small applied voltage.

Therefore, free electrons play the crucial role in conducting current in metallic materials.

title: NDA General Ability 4 September 2022
question: What is the nature of velocity-time graph for a car moving with uniform acceleration?
solution:
The nature of the velocity-time graph for a car moving with uniform acceleration is a straight line. This is because uniform acceleration means that the velocity of the car changes at a constant rate over time.

Mathematically, if a car is moving with uniform acceleration, the relationship between its velocity $ v $ and time $ t $ can be described by the equation:

$$ v = u + at $$

Where:

  • $ v $ is the final velocity.
  • $ u $ is the initial velocity.
  • $ a $ is the constant acceleration.
  • $ t $ is the time.

This equation is of the form of a linear equation $ y = mx + c $, where $ m $ (the slope) represents the acceleration $ a $, and $ c $ (the y-intercept) represents the initial velocity $ u $. Therefore, the graph of velocity versus time will be a straight line.

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option C: Straight line

question: The amplitude of sound waves is measured in the units of
solution:
The correct answer is Option A: pressure. Here's why:

Sound waves are longitudinal waves, meaning they travel by compressing and expanding the medium they pass through. The amplitude of a sound wave refers to the maximum displacement of the particles in the medium from their equilibrium position. This displacement directly corresponds to the change in pressure caused by the wave.

Let's break down why the other options are incorrect:

  • Distance: While distance is related to the wavelength of a sound wave (the distance between two consecutive crests or troughs), it doesn't represent the amplitude.
  • Time: Time is related to the frequency of a sound wave (the number of waves passing a point per second), not its amplitude.
  • Speed: The speed of sound depends on the medium it travels through and is not directly related to the amplitude of the wave.

In summary, the amplitude of a sound wave is measured in units of pressure, as it represents the maximum pressure variation caused by the wave.

title: NDA General Ability 4 September 2022
question: A current of 0.6 A is drawn by an electric bulb for 10 minutes. Which one of the following is the amount of electric charge that flows through the circuit?
solution:
To determine the amount of electric charge that flows through the circuit, we can use the relationship between current, charge, and time. The equation we will use is:

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

where:

  • $ Q $ is the electric charge in coulombs (C).
  • $ I $ is the current in amperes (A).
  • $ t $ is the time in seconds (s).

Given:

  • Current $ I = 0.6 $ A
  • Time $ t = 10 $ minutes

First, we need to convert the time from minutes to seconds:

$$ t = 10 \text{ minutes} \times 60 \text{ seconds/minute} = 600 \text{ seconds} $$

Now we can substitute $ I $ and $ t $ into the equation:

$$ Q = 0.6 \text{ A} \times 600 \text{ s} = 360 \text{ C} $$

Therefore, the amount of electric charge that flows through the circuit is 360 C. Thus, the correct option is:

Option C: 360 C

title: NDA General Ability 4 September 2022
question: A DC generator works on the principle of
solution:
A DC generator works on the principle of:

Option C

Faraday’s laws of electromagnetic induction

Explanation:

Faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction are fundamental principles that describe how electric currents can be induced in conductors due to changing magnetic fields. A DC generator converts mechanical energy into electrical energy using these principles. When a conductor (such as a coil of wire) moves through a magnetic field, it cuts through the field lines, resulting in an induced electromotive force (EMF) or voltage in the conductor. This phenomenon is described by Faraday's laws, which state:

  • First Law: Any change in the magnetic field within a closed loop of wire will induce an EMF in the wire.
  • Second Law: The magnitude of the induced EMF is directly proportional to the rate of change of the magnetic flux through the loop.

Therefore, a DC generator operates based on these laws to produce a continuous direct current (DC) output.
